biblical-hebrew.reconstruction_method: Tiberian Masoretic vocalization + medieval treatises + comparative Semitic (Khan primary)
biblical-hebrew.scholarly_consensus: Tiberian tradition best documented and most reliable reconstruction for Biblical period with medieval continuity.

tradition_comparison.tiberian: Medieval Galilee Masoretes: full vowels, cantillation, gemination; standard for scholarship (Khan).
tradition_comparison.babylonian: Simpler vowel system; less detail on length/gutturals.
tradition_comparison.samaritan: Conservative consonants; different vowels but oral living tradition.
tradition_comparison.most_historical: Tiberian closest to Second Temple + medieval continuity; Samaritan valuable for archaic gutturals.

vowels: a e i o u + length/reduced via niqqud (sheva mobile/silent)
consonants: bgdkpt (begadkefat spirantization with/without dagesh); q ṣ ṭ; ʾ ḥ ʿ; š ś s z; r l m n w y
phoneme_inventory.distinctive_features: Dagesh forte/lene; matres lectionis; spirantization; pharyngeals preserved.

ipa_reconstructed.confidence_note: Khan reconstruction + Foreman performances; comparative Semitic strengthens gutturals/pharyngeals.

biblical-hebrew.stress_rules: Penultimate or ultimate; cantillation dictates phrasing/stress.
biblical-hebrew.cantillation_notes: Ta'amim marks guide melody, pauses, emphasis; critical for authentic pacing and liturgical flow.
biblical-hebrew.grok_imagine_guidance: Gutturals: ʿayin deep pharyngeal scrape [ʕ], ḥet voiceless [ħ]; resh uvular/trill; sheva schwa or zero; dagesh doubles or hardens; cantillation for natural chant-like rises/falls and emphasis.
tutoring_hooks: Tiberian Masoretes preserved pronunciation meticulously in Galilee.
Gutturals distinguish meaning — largely lost in Modern Hebrew but key for authenticity.
Cantillation is ancient musical notation for text — like sheet music for scripture.
Begadkefat spirantization rule still echoes in Modern Hebrew.
Samaritan tradition keeps some sounds continuously alive.
sources: Khan *The Tiberian Pronunciation Tradition*; Joüon-Muraoka; Ben-Hayyim Samaritan; Foreman recitations; comparative Semitic.

classical-sanskrit.reconstruction_method: Shiksha texts — ancient native phonetics treatises
classical-sanskrit.scholarly_consensus: Extremely high precision due to indigenous Shiksha/Pāṇini analysis unmatched until modern era.
classical-sanskrit.shiksha_notes: Pāṇinīya Śikṣā and Prātiśākhya describe exact articulation places/manners (retroflex curl, aspirate breath burst, length, sandhi); vowels pure cardinal; 52 akshara system.

vowels: a ā i ī u ū ṛ ḷ e ai o au (pure with length)
consonants: Stops: 5 vargas k/kh g/gh ṅ etc.; retroflex ṭ/ṭh ḍ/ḍh ṇ; dental t/th d/dh n; labial p/ph b/bh m
retroflex_series: ṭ ṭh ḍ ḍh ṇ ṣ ṛ — tongue curled back
aspirate_pairs: Plain vs aspirated throughout (ph bh gh etc. — breathy release)
phoneme_inventory.distinctive_features: Voicing + aspiration; retroflex vs dental; full nasals/sibilants; anusvāra/visarga.

ipa_reconstructed.confidence_note: Shiksha/Pāṇini + living Vedic recitation confirms every detail.

classical-sanskrit.stress_rules: Quantitative (syllable weight long/short); Vedic pitch accents (udātta etc.).

prosody.vedic_accent_note: Pitch accents (high/low/falling) preserved in traditional recitation.

classical-sanskrit.grok_imagine_guidance: Retroflex curl for ṭ/ḍ/ṣ (tongue tip back); strong aspirate bursts (ph/bh); pure cardinal long vowels (hold twice); exact length distinctions; sandhi euphony.
tutoring_hooks: Shiksha texts provide more detailed phonetics than Western linguistics until 19th century.
Devanagari is one of the most perfectly phonetic scripts ever devised.
Aspirates and retroflexes create a distinctive 'clicky' precision.
Unbroken pandit recitation tradition keeps pronunciation alive.
Pāṇini grammar + Shiksha represent pinnacle of ancient linguistic analysis.
sources: Shiksha treatises (Pāṇinīya etc.); Whitney/MacDonell; Cardona on Pāṇini; living Vedic recitation traditions.

akkadian.reconstruction_method: Cuneiform sign values + bilinguals + Semitic comparative (Huehnergard/von Soden)
akkadian.scholarly_consensus: Strong on consonants/vowel length from signs; emphatics debated (ejective vs pharyngealized).

vowels: a e i u short/long; mimation endings
consonants: p b t d k g q ṣ ṭ; s z š; m n l r w y ḥ ʾ
emphatic_series: ṭ q ṣ (pharyngealized or ejective; Geers' law evidence)

ipa_reconstructed.sample_text: Enuma Elish approx 'When on high heaven and earth'
ipa_reconstructed.confidence_note: Strong on signs; conventional emphatics; dialect shifts noted.

akkadian.grok_imagine_guidance: Emphatics as throaty or popped ejectives; hold long vowels; mimation final m; glottal aleph; cuneiform context for polyphony.
tutoring_hooks: Lingua franca of ancient Near East for 2,000+ years — diplomatic 'English'.
Cuneiform signs multi-value — spelling reveals consonants clearly.
Emphatics distinguish from Hebrew/Arabic cousins.
Influenced Aramaic/Hebrew phonology heavily.
Enuma Elish uses rhythmic parallelism like epic poetry.
sources: Huehnergard *Grammar of Akkadian*; von Soden; CDLI; Semitic comparative linguistics.

sumerian.reconstruction_method: Akkadian transcriptions + internal spelling + Emesal contrast (Jagersma/Thomsen)
sumerian.scholarly_consensus: Tentative and lowest certainty; many phonemes inferred/indirect.
sumerian.emesal_notes: Priestly/women's dialect: softer/different consonants/vowels; contrast reveals standard realizations via Akkadian lens.

vowels: a e i u (length/quality uncertain; harmony hints)
consonants: p b t d g k; s š z; m n l r; ĝ (ng?/velar); ḫ
disputed_phonemes: ĝ (nasal or stop?), r (tap/trill/affricate?), extra sibilants; no confirmed /f h/
phoneme_inventory.distinctive_features: Ergative; heavy compounding/logograms; isolate with no relatives.

ipa_reconstructed.confidence_note: Sumerian pronunciation is the least certain of any language in the DAVID registry — heavily inferred through Akkadian filter.

sumerian.grok_imagine_guidance: Uncertain r/ĝ approximate as nasal/stop/tap; vowel harmony suggestions; heavy logographic compounding slow pacing; ergative endings; Akkadian-like sibilants for approximation.
tutoring_hooks: World's oldest attested language (c. 3200 BCE cuneiform) — true isolate.
Emesal dialect provides clues through contrast with standard.
Logograms vs phonetic complements create complex script.
Akkadian scribes give us our main reconstruction window.
Liturgical influence survived language death in Babylonian religion.
sources: Jagersma *Descriptive Grammar*; Thomsen; ETCSL; CDLI bilinguals.
